This was fifth day so we put in a thoracoscope by removing the chest tube along the same tract which was in fact the sixth phase in the posterior axillary line.
So here the chest tube has gone in and there are lots of additions.
So initially we do a pleural lysis with the scope itself by staying close to the chest wall.
We are doing a bit of adhesial lysis and as you can see we have tried to create a space for the axillary trochar which we are palpating in between the ribs.
So axillary one was in the
fourth space on the anterior axillary line.
You can see a loculated abscess down below slightly inferior and the way to localize was we put in a needle at the spot we needed to avoid the intercostal arteries followed by a 5 mm troca.
So here we go in and
To cut the story short, I waited it and we entered not only the abscess cavity below but also we entered the upper abscess which was not accessible to the chest tube.
and here I put in a 5 mm instrument and the view inside was not great so we have interchanged the positions so by bringing the 5 mm telescope through the anterior axillary line

and we entered this abscess cavity and enlarged it the opening with the help of the suction tube itself avoiding diathermy as far as possible because on the fifth day you know there can be lots of additions so there is thick pus coming through the upper cavity this is the part that had not been entered by the chest tube so we go and aspirate it

Now that the pus was quite thick so it wouldn't come that easily so there was a lot of problem we had to keep taking the suction tube out because the tube was getting blocked the suction tube was getting blocked the rigid suction tube and 10 mm was sucking too fast.
And you can see the abscess cavity down below as well.
Now we have made it into one abscess cavity by opening the, by making the opening larger.
You can see the pericardial pulsations, the pulsation of the cardiac pulsation which can be seen through the pericardium.
We did a bit of adhesiolysis here but as you know we cannot go too much more medial because the inferior thoracic, inferior mammary
internal mammary artery will be visualized.
So we have started irrigating to make sure that the pus is a bit thicker and easier to aspirate.

So unlike the abdomen when you suck there is not too much of a collapse of the cavity because the lung is a bit thick here for it to come up that easily and the cavity is quite rigid it doesn't collapse.
So here we go in, do multiple aspirations.
We can also put in antibiotic solutions, but here ours was a plain saline irrigation, sterile saline irrigation.
Our strategy was to keep aspirating and look for any other openings.
Here you can see posteriorly there is abscess coming out.
So the advantage of having an electronic table is the table is tilted forwards so that the abscess is drained quite well.
Because this is not an easy space for it
Like in the abdomen here you have a rigid cavity so you need the positions of the table also to be tilted and here we've taken as much of the abscess as possible.
And then we try to do more adhesiolysis with a standard thoracoscopic instrument, which is a curved instrument.
We are trying to use a hook here with diathermy.
But as you know, it's not very easy to put in the localized space to put in a curved instrument.
And we somehow prefer that the straight instruments are better.
And we are doing a decortication, the limited decortication of the adherent part of the lung to the chest wall.

Doing this just to make sure there is no other abscess cavity and that other posterior cavity that was seen was seen quite well as in the last few seconds of the film and here we put in the chest tube and the chest tube is curved up into the upper and the lower abscess cavity some of the holes are sitting in the lower cavity.
whereas the tip and a few other holes are sitting in the upper cavity, thus straining both the chest tubes.

At this stage, we have the anesthetist who had initially inserted a double lumen tube to open up the left lung as well, and here she is opening up the left lung with a bag in the hands, nicely opening the chest wall, which is chest.
